source_intake_record.raw_question_text: Many of the stars in the sky are actually binary stars, in which two stars orbit about their common center of mass. If the orbital speeds of the stars are high enough, the motion of the stars can be detected by the Doppler shifts of the light they emit. Stars for which this is the case are called \emph{spectroscopic binary stars}. Figure shows the simplest case of a spectroscopic binary star: two identical stars orbiting their center of mass in a circle. The plane of the stars' orbits is edge-on to the line of sight of an observer on the earth. The light from each star in the binary system varies from its maximum frequency to its minimum frequency and back again in 11.0 days. Determine the orbital radius \( R \) and the mass \( m \) of each star. Give your answer for \( m \) in kilograms and as a multiple of the mass of the sun, \( 1.99 \times 10^{30} \) kg. Compare the value of \( R \) to the distance from the earth to the sun, \( 1.50 \times 10^{11} \) m.

image_dependency: question_text explicitly references a figure describing the binary-star geometry
requires_image=true and text_dominant=false suggest nontrivial visual reliance
however, the main orbital configuration is also described in text, so image dependence is not scored near 1.0
multi_step: question asks for multiple physical quantities (orbital radius R and stellar mass m)
solving would require combining periodic motion/orbital relations with Doppler-based speed information
the long physics word problem and edge-on circular-orbit setup indicate multi-step reasoning rather than single-step lookup
verifiability: answer_type=short_text and registry_passed=true normally support checkability
but question_text asks for R and m, while answer_text is a single speed value (3.94 x 10^4 m/s), indicating a likely question-answer mismatch
multiple-choice options are also speeds, reinforcing that the recorded answer target may not match the stated prompt

decision_reasons: Question, image, and answer assets are all present, so the sample retains recoverable core semantics.
Initial scoring flags a likely question-answer mismatch: the prompt appears to ask for orbital radius and stellar mass, while the recorded answer is a single speed value.
There is additional risk of an incomplete or misaligned prompt and possible image-carried critical information, which lowers confidence for immediate inclusion.
Following high-recall collection policy, these issues justify deprioritization rather than rejection.
